Chronology

Interactive Timeline

From the classical revival to computational design — the styles in sequence. Click any entry to open it.

-400

Classical

Antiquity (8th c. BCE – 4th c. CE) · Greece · Rome — The original orders — column, pediment and proportion.
0

Vernacular

Timeless / pre-modern · Global (place-specific) — Built by tradition from local materials and climate.
1450

Renaissance

15th – 16th century · Italy → Europe — The rebirth of classical order and human proportion.
1600

Baroque

17th – 18th century · Italy → Europe — Movement, drama and grandeur in stone.
1790

Neo-Classical

1750s – 1900s (revived since) · Europe → Global — Traditional, formal, symmetrical — columns and proportion.
1840

Gothic Revival

1740 – 1900 · Britain → Global — Soaring pointed arches and medieval romance reborn.
1850

Victorian

1837 – 1901 · Britain → Global — Exuberant eclectic ornament and picturesque silhouettes.
1850

Romanesque Revival

1840 – 1900 · USA · Europe — Heavy stone, round arches and robust solidity.
1860

Beaux-Arts

1850 – 1920 · France → USA — Monumental classical grandeur, richly composed.
1880

Colonial Revival

1880 – 1955 · USA — Refined symmetry recalling the colonial Georgian house.
1895

Art Nouveau

1890 – 1910 · Europe — Nature flowing into line, curve and ornament.
1900

Prairie Style

1900 – 1920 · USA Midwest — Long low horizontals married to the open prairie.
1925

Modern

1920s – 1970s · Europe → Global — Clean lines, functional forms, no ornament.
1925

Art Deco

1920s – 1940s · France → USA → Global — Glamorous, geometric, decorative — strong lines and symmetry.
1935

Organic Architecture

1900s – present · USA · Global — Building and landscape as one continuous whole.
1945

Desert Modernism

1940s – 1970s · USA Southwest — Mid-century modernism shaped by sun, heat and the desert.
1945

Mid-Century Modern

1945 – 1970 · USA · Global — Clean lines, indoor–outdoor connection.
1950

Scandinavian

1950s – present · Nordic Europe — Simplicity, functionality, nature.
1955

Brutalism

1950s – 1980s · Europe → Global — Bold, massive, sculptural — exposed raw concrete.
1975

High-Tech

1970s – present · UK · Global — The structure and services are the architecture.
1985

Mediterranean Contemporary

1980s – present · Mediterranean · Global — Warm, relaxed, timeless — stucco walls and clean forms.
1990

Minimalism

1980s – present · Global — Simplicity — 'less is more'.
1990

Tropical Modern

1990s – present · Tropics · Global — Indoor–outdoor living, climate responsive.
1990

Deconstructivism

1980s – present · Global — Experimental, dynamic, bold — fragmented, non-linear form.
1995

Japanese Minimalism

1990s – present · Japan — Harmony, simplicity, nature.
2000

Industrial

1990s – present · Global — Raw, urban, utilitarian — exposed structure, big windows.
2005

Contemporary

2000s – present · Global — Current trends, evolving — mixed materials, clean geometry.
2010

Biophilic

2010s – present · Global — Architecture woven with living nature.
2010

Parametric

2000s – present · Global — Fluid, futuristic, innovative — curves and complex geometry.
2010

Sustainable / Passive

2000s – present · Global — Eco-friendly, efficient, harmonious — green roofs and living façades.

Architecture Atlas

A practical, exterior-focused architectural design reference — choose a direction and architectural language for any project. Built for design teams.
